title: US Central Command Reports 49 Commercial Ships Turned Back Under Iran Port Blockade
summary: US Central Command confirmed that 49 commercial ships have been directed to turn back from Iranian ports since the naval blockade commenced on April 13, intensifying economic pressure on Tehran.
contents:
## US Naval Blockade Tightens on Iranian Ports

### Blockade Status
US Central Command (CENTCOM) confirmed on May 3 that 49 commercial ships have received orders to turn back from Iranian ports since the naval blockade commenced on April 13. The figure represents an increase from 48 ships reported the previous day and 45 ships reported by Friday.

### Economic Impact
The blockade aims to deprive Tehran of oil revenue critical to sustaining its war effort and economy. US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Fox News that Iran has collected less than $1.3 million in Hormuz tolls, characterizing it as "a pittance on their previous daily oil revenues."

### Oil Storage Reaching Capacity
Bessent warned that Iran's oil storage fills rapidly, projecting that Tehran would need to "start shutting in wells" within the next week. This would represent a significant escalation in economic pressure, potentially forcing Iran to permanently reduce production capacity.

### Toll Enforcement
The US simultaneously warned shipping companies they could face sanctions for paying Iran tolls for Strait of Hormuz transit. Iran's Deputy Parliament Speaker Ali Nikzad declared Tehran "will not back down" from its position on the strait and that conditions would not return to pre-war status.

### Strategic Balance
The dual blockade situation — Iran restricting Hormuz transit while the US blocks Iranian ports — has created an unprecedented standoff in the world's most critical oil shipping corridor.

**Sources:** AP News, RFE/RL, Fox News
